_meta.description: Persistent state for the Rule of Life Planner. Each top-level key matches a localStorage key in the browser; values are stringified JSON.

rol-evening-v1:
[{"date":"2026-05-02","savedAt":"2026-05-03T01:30:00.000Z","answers":{"ev-gratitude":"I am thankful for a productive retreat today and a safe trip home.","ev-consolation":"I felt most alive while navigating hard questions and also hearing what makes our diocese so special.","ev-desolation":"I felt resistant to brush the teeth of the kids. It was so simple, but I was so tired and wanted to sit down for a little longer. I was snappy for no particular reason.","ev-tomorrow":"Presence and intention. I want to be present at church while being intentional about moving through the post-church meetings so I can be present with Brooklyn at her flag football game."},"reflection":"There's a quiet symmetry in tonight's noticings. The consolation rises in something hard — sitting with diocesan questions that don't yield easy answers, while also receiving what makes this place beloved. That's an Ignatian movement worth marking: the Spirit often consoles us not by removing the weight but by enlarging the love that bears it. You came alive inside the difficulty, not after it.

The desolation, by contrast, was small and human — a snappy edge at the toothbrushing hour, the body's tax on a long day of travel. The Desert tradition would not call that sin so much as a logismos, a passing thought that catches us when we're depleted. Cassian's counsel in the Conferences is gentle: notice it, name it, but don't let it accuse you. Brooklyn and your other little ones received a tired father tonight; tomorrow they will receive a present one.

Which is exactly what your tomorrow names: presence and intention. Not to be everywhere, but to be fully where you are — at the altar first, then through the post-church meetings with disciplined kindness, and then on the sideline of a flag football game where holiness will look like a folding chair and your full attention on Brooklyn.

May your sleep be deep, and may you wake to mercy."},{"date":"2026-05-03","savedAt":"2026-05-04T01:30:00.000Z","answers":{"ev-gratitude":"My family and our love and connection to one another. Cooking with the kids, specifically introducing them to pizza date night, as Vanessa and I used to call it. Watching Top Chef on the couch. It is really special.","ev-consolation":"I really felt most connected to God in two places. First, during Youth Sunday while watching all the young people participate and their enthusiasm. Secondly, when Lennox and Brooklyn came to the rail to receive communion from their dad.","ev-desolation":"I just felt tired and I am worried that I did something in my sleep or diet that set me into that moment. Or, I just was sleepy which is also normal.","ev-tomorrow":"Really want to bring pause and reflection to my events for tomorrow. Really want to focus on what is in front of me and bring that awareness to the new day."},"reflection":"Tonight's consolations gathered around children. The parish's young at Youth Sunday, and your own at the rail. This morning's reflection named two fatherhoods, the priestly and the paternal, as a single calling worth holding together; tonight the Spirit answered by bringing them into one set of hands. Lennox and Brooklyn receiving the Body from their dad is the kind of consolation Ignatius would mark with a star: not a feeling manufactured, but a movement given (Spiritual Exercises, §316). The same anchor you named at dawn, John 14, was already preparing this. "In my Father's house are many rooms." The altar rail is one of them.

The desolation you named was a quieter, smaller thing — bodily tiredness whose cause you couldn't quite locate. The Desert tradition would not rush to interpret it. Cassian counseled discernment, not anxiety, about the body's weather (Conferences I.20). Sometimes weariness is moral, sometimes it is just sleep undone. The grace is that the same day held a pizza-pan ritual with the kids and Top Chef on the couch — a homely table that quietly converts a tired evening into communion of another kind. You were fed there too.

Tomorrow's intention — pause, reflection, presence to what is in front of you — sits in clean alignment with this morning's watchfulness against "unneeded extra conversations." These are the same posture from two angles: the discipline of staying where you actually are. Abba Moses told a brother to sit in his cell and let the cell teach him everything (Apophthegmata Patrum, Moses 6). Your cell tomorrow will be a calendar, but the lesson is the same. May your sleep be deep, and may you wake to mercy."}]
rol-examen-v2:
[{"date":"2026-05-01","savedAt":"2026-05-01T08:00:00.000Z","answers":{"ex-anchor":"1 Chronicles 16:34 (NIV): "Give thanks to the Lord, for he is good; his love endures forever."

This phrase met me on the morning I finished my Prolon fast — feeling really amazing in the body, and the verse rose as the body's own thanksgiving.","ex-gratitude":"1. Vanessa — for her support that got me through the Prolon fast cleanly, without cheating.
2. The fast itself, and how I feel in this moment.
3. The love of my children and the ways they love on me.","ex-big3":"1. Live into my post-Prolon meal plan — avoid sugar; reinsert vegetables and proteins for dinner.
2. This evening, work on magazine articles for the upcoming Field Guide edition — capture the baptism stories and write the article.
3. Continue to walk with Keith and deepen that bond.","ex-people":"Nana, Pat Davis, Abigail Lake, Kevin, Noelle, the Vestry, the Parish; Vanessa and Beaches Dermatology (something unfinished there); a smooth future at Aesthetix Plus for Vanessa; the kiddos.","ex-forward":"Massage and Reiki with Leona — to walk with her and be present with her. Also: seeing if I can complete this one-night road trip on mostly electric miles in the plug-in hybrid.","ex-watchful":"The temptation to eat dirty and eat sweets in the hotel by myself — which would throw away all the work I did. Combat with prayer, scripture, and work. Practical reminder: when the temptation rises, work on Sunday's sermon.","ex-body":"Massage, stretch, and re-enter slowly into my active life as I increase my calories."},"reflection":"There is a coherence to today's examen worth noticing. Your anchoring text (1 Chronicles 16:34 — "his love endures forever") is the body's own thanksgiving after the fast. Your gratitude names Vanessa's quiet companionship in that same fast. Your Big 3 begins with continuing the discipline of post-Prolon eating. Your watchfulness names exactly the hotel-room temptation that could undo it — and your remedy isn't willpower, it's substitution: sermon prep as the alternative to sweets. Cassian writes about this very thing in Conferences — the watchful monk doesn't fight a thought, he replaces it with a better one.

The intercessions are full and tender — Nana, Pat Davis, Abigail Lake, the staff, the Vestry, and the unsettled vocational ground beneath Vanessa's feet. That last one rises to the top, I think. Worth holding gently across the day.

May the road trip's quiet hours be a small Sabbath inside the work, and may Leona's hands meet a body that has just been remade."},{"date":"2026-05-02","savedAt":"2026-05-02T11:30:00.000Z","answers":{"ex-anchor":"The Examination of a Bishop in the Book of Common Prayer.","ex-gratitude":"The delegates who chose to show up today from the Santa Fe and River Regions.","ex-big3":"Be present. Be honest. Be hopeful. Be brief. And: prepare for tomorrow's formation class.","ex-people":"The Standing Committee and the election of our 9th Bishop. Beaches Dermatology and Summer. Clarity for Vanessa.","ex-forward":"A quiet, productive Delegates retreat and a safe trip home.","ex-watchful":"Bad food choices. Really need to make good choices today.","ex-body":"Keep up the post-Prolon diet."},"reflection":"The day's seven answers hum at one frequency: episcopal vocation. The anchor is the Examination of a Bishop (BCP, p. 517) — the questions a bishop-elect must answer about guarding the faith, unity, and discipline of the Church. The gratitude is for the delegates who will help the Diocese answer those very questions in late 2026. The people on your heart begin with the Standing Committee and the 9th Bishop. Even your Big 3 — present, honest, hopeful, brief — read less like tasks and more like dispositions you would want any bishop-elect to embody in the room you are about to hold.

What rises to the top is the discipline of restraint. "Be brief" is its own kind of fast, and it sits in coherent company with the post-Prolon diet and the watchfulness about food. Cassian, in the Institutes (Book V, on gluttony), treats the small discipline of the table as rehearsal for every larger battle the soul will face — the body's restraint and the tongue's restraint train the same heart. Today's temptation may be to over-explain, to manage outcomes, to fill silence. The Examination's own questions are answered as much by what you do not say in the retreat as by what you do.

So: presence over performance. The delegates showed up to be heard; let your brevity be hospitality. Hold Vanessa's clarity and the diocese's discernment in the same prayer — both are forms of waiting on the Spirit. May the road home be quiet, the table tomorrow well-prepared, and your heart, in the Desert phrase, watchful and unhurried."},{"date":"2026-05-03","savedAt":"2026-05-03T12:00:00.000Z","answers":{"ex-anchor":"John 14:1-14","ex-gratitude":"I am grateful for my seniors and their work within the church. I am grateful for my Standing Committee colleagues. I am grateful for my family and their support, with special emphasis on their love of reading.","ex-big3":"Get to Flag Football. Speed up meeting with Eddie and Terry. Visit with Seniors. One last one and this may be the biggest which is keep Courtney and Bryan, who cheated on Courtney, from impacting their kids with their tension.","ex-people":"Still praying for Summer at Beaches Dermatology and for clarity around Vanessa and her possible employment at Beaches Dermatology. I am praying for Nana as he prepares to bury her cousin. I am praying for Courtney and Bryan for peace and reconciliation. I am praying for the Lakes that we can find connection again.","ex-forward":"Watching Brooklyn pull one flag at her game!! I know she can do it.","ex-watchful":"Unneeded extra conversations.","ex-body":"Keep the good food choices going."},"reflection":"It is striking that you anchored yourself this morning in John 14, the passage Jesus speaks precisely because hearts are troubled. The day you described is full of troubled hearts: Courtney and Bryan's children caught between their parents' rupture, Nana walking toward a burial, the Lakes whose connection feels distant, Vanessa standing inside a question about Beaches Dermatology. Before any of that crossed your desk, the Word had already gone before you: "Let not your hearts be troubled... I am the way." Your anchor is doing pastoral work before the day even begins.

What rises to the top is children. You named protecting Courtney and Bryan's kids from their parents' tension as the biggest of your Big 3, and the delight you are looking for today is Brooklyn pulling a flag. The same morning holds two fatherhoods: the priestly one, where you stand between other people's children and the wreckage of adult choices, and the paternal one, where you simply get to cheer. Hold them together. They are not unrelated callings; one teaches the other.

Your watchfulness, "unneeded extra conversations," is the old Desert wisdom rephrased. Abba Arsenius said he had often regretted his speech but never his silence (Apophthegmata Patrum, Arsenius 40), and Cassian made nepsis, watchfulness of the heart, central to the Christian life (Conferences I.5). The discipline you are naming is not aloofness. It is the conservation of pastoral attention so that the words you do speak today, to the Seniors, to Eddie and Terry, perhaps to Courtney or Bryan, carry the weight they need to carry. May the Lord keep your heart untroubled, your speech sparing, your love wide, and may Brooklyn pull that flag clean off."}]
